retrieved_docs.title: Oral Flap Design -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Anatomical Structures of the Maxilla
retrieved_docs.content: The facial artery, a proximal branch of the external carotid system, supplies the superficial structures of the face, including facial expression muscles, skin, and facial fat and fascia. This artery crosses the mandible at the gonial notch to enter the face, traveling deeply along the surfaces of the buccinator and levator anguli oris muscles as it approaches the oral commissure. At this point, it divides into the inferior and superior labial arteries, with the superior labial artery giving rise to the angular artery, which supplies the lateral nose (see Image. Anatomy of Facial Artery).

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The upper and lower lips are known as, respectively, labium superius oris and labium inferius oris. Both the upper and lower lip contain mucosal membrane, vermilion, and cutaneous surfaces. While considerations of the lips are often centered on the vermilion zone, the upper lip extends from the nasolabial folds to the inferior margin of the nose, and the lower lip encompasses the region between the lateral commissures and the labiomental crease of the chin. The upper and lower lips intersect at the mouth angle, referred to as the commissure. This is the point at which several muscles involved in lip movement attach.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The lips are an essential aspect of the human face and play a critical role in facial expression, phonation, sensation, mastication, physical attraction, and intimacy. The upper and lower lips are known as, respectively, labium superius oris and labium inferius oris. Both the upper and lower lip contains mucosal membrane, vermilion, and cutaneous surfaces. This article briefly reviews the anatomy, neurovasculature, and musculature of the lips, as well as important surgical and clinical considerations regarding lip pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: Facial Reconstruction for Mohs Defect Repairs -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Subunits: The upper lip divides into the philtral subunit and two lateral subunits, bounded by the melolabial creases laterally. The lower lip involves one subunit, separated from the chin by the mental crease. The vermillion border represents the mucocutaneous junction of the lips, with the superior and inferior red lips (i.e., mucous membrane) representing individual subunits. Layers: from superficial to deep, this includes the skin, muscle (e.g., orbicularis oris muscle), and mucosa. Blood supply: superior and inferior labial arteries that normally travel deep to the orbicularis oris muscle

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Structure and Function -- Upper Face
retrieved_docs.content: The forehead is the superior region of the upper face region. The superficial layer of the forehead is made up of skin. Deeper to the skin layer of the forehead is the fat pads. The central forehead fat pad is in the center of the forehead. Laterally to the central forehead pad is the middle forehead fat pad. The middle forehead fat pad is superior to the orbits. The fat pad that makes up the most lateral region of the upper face is the lateral temporal fat pad. The lateral temporal fat pad overlies the temporal regions and spans inferiorly to the angle of the mandible.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Face -- Structure and Function -- Middle Face
retrieved_docs.content: The cheeks are lateral to the nose. The cheeks are covered with skin superficially, but deep to the skin, the cheeks contain a lot of fat pads. The most medial fat pad in the cheek region that demarcates the nose from the cheek is the nasolabial cheek fat pad. Just lateral to the middle cheek fat is the superficial medial cheek fat. The superficial medial cheek fat is directly inferior to the inferior orbital fat pad. More laterally, the middle cheek fat pad is lateral to the superficial cheek fat pad. The superficial cheek fat pad meets the inferior extension of the lateral temporal fat pad. The cheek region also contains a buccal fat pad that overlies the buccal muscle.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The vermilion of the lips is comprised of a modified mucous membrane composed of hairless, highly vascularized, stratified squamous epithelium. This membrane is three to five cellular layers in thickness, in contrast to the 16-layer facial skin. Additionally, the vermilion lacks the typical skin appendages seen in the cutaneous lip, bearing no hair follicles or salivary, sweat, and sebaceous glands. The vermilion ranges in color from reddish-pink to brown, depending on ethnicity. The characteristic redness of the vermilion zone is secondary to the paucity of melanocytes and high density of superficial vasculature underlying the membrane. Thus, the blood vessels appear prominent, particularly in those with lighter skin. The red line denotes the division between the dry vermilion and the mucous membrane of the oral cavity in both the upper and lower lips. The transition to the wet labial mucosal is marked by the presence of submucosal salivary glands and the sudden cessation of skin lines.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Scar lines should be oriented within the natural boundary lines if possible or angled radially along the relaxed skin tension lines to minimize visibility. As notching is a common outcome of surgical interventions to the lips, attempts should be made to avoid scar lines that extend across the vermilion-cutaneous junction. Repair of full-thickness lip defects should occur in four layers (submucosa, orbicularis oris, vermilion border, cutaneous lip), utilizing small-caliber 5-0 or 6-0 sutures. Given the ability of silk sutures to absorb fluid and soften, they are recommended for surgical closure of the vermilion or mucosa. Given the tendency of scars on the lip to depress, wound edges should be aggressively everted during suturing to ensure that the scar will lay flat after healing.

retrieved_docs.title: Direct Brow Lift -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Pertinent Anatomic Structures
retrieved_docs.content: Supraorbital nerve This nerve exits a notch or foramen roughly 2.7 cm lateral to the midline forehead between the midpupillary line and the medial limbus. The supraorbital nerve further divides into deep and superficial branches, providing sensory innervation to the frontoparietal and upper eyelid skin, respectively. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Structure and Function
retrieved_docs.content: The upper lip is characterized by a symmetrical pair of paramedian vertical philtral ridges bordering the central depression known as the philtrum, directly below the nasal septum. The philtral ridges and the philtrum are formed by a unique collection of dermal collagen and dense elastic tissue. As elasticity is diminished with age, the philtrum takes on a less prominent appearance. The philtrum is believed to serve as a supply of additional skin to be recruited for oral movements requiring stretching of the upper lip. The inferior margin of the philtrum forms the downward arch of the cupid’s bow, while the underlying fleshy fullness is known as the tubercle or procheilon. Outlining the vermilion borders of the upper and lower lips is a 2 to 3 mm pale convexity known as the white roll, formed by the bulging of the orbicularis oris muscle lying beneath. The upper and lower lips connect to the gums by the frenulum labii superioris and frenulum labii inferioris, respectively.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: Options for surgical closure of lip defects include primary repair, secondary intent, and flap closure. Primary repair is indicated for defects involving less than 30% of the area of the lip. Secondary intention can be utilized for superficial wounds of the vermilion, up to 2.5 cm. Defects extending greater than 2 mm into the cutaneous lip or the orbicularis oris are at increased risk of functional and cosmetic complications. If allowing lip defects to resolve via secondary intention, patients should be closely monitored for the development of lip notching or other malalignments.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Physiologic Variants
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary to advancing age and chronic sun exposure, venous lakes may develop on the lips. These benign blanchable blue-purple macule-papules more frequently affect the lower lip. Although benign, venous lakes may be treated via surgical excision or laser therapy due to cosmetic preferences.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Submental Triangle -- Blood Supply and Lymphatics
retrieved_docs.content: The submental lymph nodes collect lymph from the central area of the lower lip, the skin of the mental region, the tip of the tongue, and the incisor teeth. Next, they drain into the submandibular lymph nodes and the deep cervical group, which eventually drains into the jugular lymph trunk.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lateral Nasal Artery -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: As mentioned, the blood supply to the external nose is complicated and redundant, which permits reliable healing and infection resistance even after extensive trauma or surgical intervention. The anterior lateral nasal arteries represent the primary perfusion to the tip of the nose. Without their contribution to blood flow, dividing the columellar artery during open rhinoplasty would likely lead to more healing complications. [1] Likewise, dermal fillers may compress small arteries and cause soft tissue ischemia when injected into the nose. Still, the collateral circulation afforded by the lateral nasal arteries and other branches often prevents severe complications. [11] The anterior lateral nasal artery also passes underneath the levator labii superioris alaeque nasi muscle and nourishes it; this muscle can be used as a regional flap to reconstruct full-thickness nasal defects. [9] The commonly used nasolabial flap has also been described based on the anterior lateral nasal artery in addition to its often employed random blood supply; the nasolabial flap has myriad applications within the midface but is most commonly used for alar reconstruction. [12] Numerous other local and regional soft tissue flaps that derive their blood supply from the anterior lateral nasal artery or its branches have also been described; they are particularly useful for lateral nasal wall, columellar, and alar reconstruction. [13] [4] [14]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck, Lips -- Surgical Considerations
retrieved_docs.content: An important goal of lip reconstruction is to maintain the competence of the oral seal to allow for mastication and phonation. Surgical repair of lesions of the lips poses a specific challenge as significant aesthetic defects are possible with even minor disruptions of the natural vermilion border, cupid’s bow, and white roll. All incisions should be limited to within a single cosmetic unit to minimize the appearance of scars. The cosmetic units of the lips include a single unit comprising the lower lip and 1 medial and 2 lateral units of the upper lip. [10] [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 182
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Skin provides a detailed hierarchical framework for metastatic malignant neoplasms of the skin, collectively termed “الورم النقيلي” (metastatic tumor). It enumerates child entities according to precise cutaneous locations, including the abdomen, axilla, back, breast, buttock, cheek, chest, chin, ear, elbow, external auditory canal, eyebrow, eyelid, face, finger, forearm, forehead, groin, hand, lip, lower limb, neck, perianal skin, perineum, scalp, shoulder, temporal region, trunk, umbilicus, upper limb, wrist, arm, as well as specialized structures such as skin adnexa, sweat glands, and sebaceous glands. By categorizing lesions by these anatomical sites, the classification aids clinicians in recognizing patterns of cutaneous spread from internal malignancies, directing targeted biopsy and histopathologic evaluation, accurately staging disease, and formulating appropriate therapeutic and counseling strategies for patients with secondary skin involvement.

A secondary malignant neoplasm of the skin of the arm is also recognized. All of these entities are unified under the Arabic concept الورم النقيلي (metastatic tumor), with a child category ورم ذو سلوك محير في الجلد (“tumor with puzzling behavior in the skin”) highlighting diagnostic difficulty. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: The Clinical Classification of Neoplasms of the Digestive System is organized under broader parent categories that include neoplasms of unspecified nature or uncertain behavior and malignant lip cancer (سرطان الشفة الخبيث). Within this hierarchy, the entity Metastatic malignant neoplasm to sublingual gland is linked to the concept of a malignant secondary tumor in other specified sites (ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى) and is identified as a malignant tumor of the sublingual gland (ورم خبيث في الغدة تحت اللسان). The classification also aligns malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) with the same secondary‑tumor mapping, indicating that the Arabic description corresponds to malignant secondary tumors in other locations. Melanomas of the skin are recognized as a subtype of malignant skin cancer and, through an inverse hierarchical relationship, are associated with malignant lip cancer in certain taxonomies. This integrated structure demonstrates how specific tumor types such as metastatic sublingual gland neoplasms and cutaneous melanomas are nested under broader parent categories and how multilingual mappings connect equivalent diagnostic terms across English and Arabic nomenclatures.

Metastasis to head‑and‑neck lymph nodes is classified as سرطان الرأس والعنق (head and neck cancer) and also exhibits the morphology of الورم النقيلي, linking nodal spread to the same metastatic tumor concept. Metastatic malignant neoplasms to the cranial bones—including ethmoid, frontal, occipital, parietal, and temporal bones—are each identified as malignant neoplasms of the respective bone (malignant neoplasm of ethmoid bone, malignant neoplasm of frontal bone, etc.) and are associated with الورم النقيلي, reflecting metastatic involvement of the skull.
